questions.question: What is the range of the 'short' data type in Java?
questions.explanation: The short data type is a 16-bit signed integer with a range from -32768 to 32767.

questions.question: What is the output? int x = 10; x -= 3; System.out.println(x);
questions.explanation: The -= operator subtracts the right operand from the left operand. x = x - 3 = 10 - 3 = 7.

questions.question: What is the output? int[] arr = {10, 20, 30, 40}; System.out.println(arr[arr.length - 1]);
questions.explanation: arr.length is 4, so arr.length-1 is 3. arr[3] is the last element, which is 40.

questions.question: What is the result of: 20 % 6 in Java?
questions.explanation: The modulus operator (%) returns the remainder. 20 divided by 6 is 3 with a remainder of 2.

questions.question: Which primitive data type can store the value 3.14159?
questions.explanation: Floating-point numbers like 3.14159 require float or double data types. Float can store decimal values.

questions.question: What is the output? int count = 0; for(int i=0; i<10; i++) { count++; } System.out.println(count);
questions.explanation: The loop runs 10 times (i from 0 to 9), incrementing count each time. Final value is 10.

questions.question: What is the output? System.out.println(7.0 / 2);
questions.explanation: Since one operand is a double (7.0), floating-point division is performed, resulting in 3.5.

questions.question: What is the maximum value of a byte in Java?
questions.explanation: The byte data type ranges from -128 to 127, so the maximum value is 127.

questions.question: What is the output? int[] nums = new int[3]; nums[0] = 5; nums[2] = 15; System.out.println(nums[1]);
questions.explanation: nums[1] was never assigned a value, so it retains the default value of 0 for int arrays.

questions.question: What is the result of: 5 != 5 in Java?
questions.explanation: The != operator checks for inequality. Since 5 equals 5, the result is false.

questions.question: What is the output? for(int i=2; i<8; i+=3) { System.out.print(i + " "); }
questions.explanation: The loop starts at 2 and increments by 3: prints 2, then 5, then stops when i becomes 8 (not less than 8).

questions.question: Can a boolean variable be assigned null in Java?
options: No, only primitive types cannot be null
Yes, if it's a Boolean wrapper class
questions.correctAnswer: Yes, if it's a Boolean wrapper class
questions.explanation: Primitive boolean cannot be null, but the Boolean wrapper class can hold null values.

questions.question: What is the output? int x = 15; if(x >= 10 && x <= 20) System.out.println("In range"); else System.out.println("Out of range");
questions.explanation: x=15 satisfies both conditions (15>=10 and 15<=20), so "In range" is printed.

questions.question: What is the output? int[] a = {1, 2, 3}; int[] b = a; b[0] = 10; System.out.println(a[0]);
questions.explanation: Arrays are reference types. b = a makes both variables point to the same array. Changing b[0] also changes a[0].

questions.question: What is the output? int x = 5; while(x < 5) { System.out.print(x); x++; } System.out.println("Done");
questions.explanation: The condition x<5 is false initially (x=5), so the loop body never executes. Only "Done" is printed.

questions.question: What is the size of the 'char' data type in Java?
questions.explanation: The char data type is a 16-bit (2 bytes) Unicode character.

questions.question: What is the output? int x = 10; System.out.println(--x);
questions.explanation: The pre-decrement operator (--x) decrements first, then returns the value. So x becomes 9, then prints 9.

questions.question: What happens when you use 'return' inside a loop?
questions.explanation: The 'return' statement exits the entire method, not just the loop. Use 'break' to exit only the loop.

questions.question: What is the result of: !(5 > 3) in Java?
questions.explanation: 5>3 is true, and the NOT operator (!) inverts it to false.

questions.question: What is the output? String[] words = {"Java", "Python", "C++"}; System.out.println(words.length);
questions.explanation: The array has 3 elements, so words.length returns 3.

questions.question: What is the output? int num = 25; if(num % 5 == 0 && num % 2 != 0) System.out.println("Match"); else System.out.println("No Match");
questions.explanation: 25%5==0 is true (divisible by 5) and 25%2!=0 is true (not divisible by 2/odd), so "Match" is printed.

questions.question: What is the output? int[][] matrix = new int[2][3]; System.out.println(matrix[0][0]);
questions.explanation: 2D arrays are initialized with default values. For int, the default is 0.

questions.question: What is the output? for(int i=1; i<=10; i++) { if(i>5) break; if(i%2==0) continue; System.out.print(i + " "); }
questions.explanation: Loop prints odd numbers (1, 3, 5) and breaks when i becomes 6.

questions.question: What is the result of: (8 < 5) ? 100 : 200 in Java?
questions.explanation: The ternary operator evaluates the condition. Since 8<5 is false, it returns 200.

questions.question: What is the output? int j = 10; do { j--; } while(j > 10); System.out.println(j);
questions.explanation: The do-while executes once: j becomes 9. Then the condition j>10 is false, so the loop exits.

questions.question: What is the default value of a short variable in Java?
questions.explanation: The default value of a short instance variable is 0.

questions.question: What is the output? int[] data = {100, 200, 300}; for(int val : data) { System.out.print(val/100 + " "); }
questions.explanation: The enhanced for-loop divides each element by 100: 100/100=1, 200/100=2, 300/100=3.

questions.question: What is the result of: false && true?
questions.explanation: The logical AND (&&) operator returns true only if both operands are true. Since one is false, the result is false.

questions.question: What is the output? for(int i=10; i>0; i-=3) { System.out.print(i + " "); }
questions.explanation: The loop starts at 10 and decrements by 3 each time: 10, 7, 4, 1, then stops when i becomes -2.

questions.question: What is the output? int p = 12, q = 8; int result = (p < q) ? p * 2 : q * 2; System.out.println(result);
questions.explanation: The ternary operator checks if p<q. Since 12<8 is false, it returns q*2 = 8*2 = 16.
